title: With Friends Like These
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Lessons on Job 6 - With Friends Like These.txt

outline.title: Introduction: A Peek Behind the Curtains of Heaven
subpoints: Chapters 1-2 gave a rare glimpse into the heavenly court — the context the friends do not have
Job 4-27 is the largest section of the book, consisting of three cycles of dialogues between Job and his three friends
The poetic format of these chapters is Hebrew poetry of the highest order
These chapters are verbally and plenarily inspired (2 Timothy 3:16) and are profitable for doctrine, reproof, and instruction

outline.title: Who Are These Three Men?
subpoints: Eliphaz the Temanite: likely from Edom, a descendant of Esau; appears to be the leader — he speaks first, speaks most, and is named by God at the end
Bildad the Shuhite: probably from a region of Mesopotamia; blunter and more aggressive than Eliphaz
Zophar the Naamathite: the most unknown and most caustic of the three; mocks Job and tells him he deserves to suffer more
All three share a retribution theology: the righteous are always blessed, the wicked always suffer

outline.title: Retribution Theology — The Friends' Core Error
subpoints: Retribution theology holds that suffering is always directly caused by personal sin, and the righteous never suffer
Eliphaz accuses Job of hypocrisy: Job taught others that suffering equals sin, yet now refuses to apply it to himself
Bildad goes right to attack: 'Does God pervert justice?' — demanding Job confess his hidden sin
Zophar mocks Job and tells him he deserves even more suffering than he has received
John 9 debunks retribution theology: Jesus says the blind man was not blind because of anyone's sin

outline.title: The Role of the Friends in the Drama of Job
subpoints: When physical suffering failed to turn Job from God, Satan sent counselors to attack his theology
The three friends become Satan's legal team, tasked with convincing Job that God is unjust
These chapters demonstrate at length how foolish it is to claim the righteous never suffer
Paul tells Timothy that the godly should expect persecution — not that righteousness guarantees ease

outline.title: Application: Giving and Receiving Biblical Counsel
subpoints: True Spirit-filled counsel will sometimes hurt — but unlike the friends, it heals rather than destroys
The surgeon's scalpel causes pain but produces wellness; the wounds of a friend are faithful (Proverbs)
Counsel must always be judged by Scripture in context, not by what we want to hear
We must not label true friends who bring hard counsel as 'Job's friends' simply because we dislike what they say
At the same time, we must not give counsel that leads people away from God — that is Satan's agenda

summary:
This lesson introduces the longest and most theologically challenging section of the book of Job: chapters 4-27, which contain three cycles of dialogue between Job and his three friends, Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite. The teacher notes that this section is written in Hebrew poetry and is fully inspired by God (2 Timothy 3:16), though interpreting it is complex because God later tells the friends they were wrong without always specifying exactly where.

The session covers who the three friends are — their likely origins, their personalities and rhetorical styles, and their shared theological framework: retribution theology. This is the idea that the righteous are always materially blessed and the wicked always suffer, making suffering a sure sign of personal sin. Each friend represents a slightly different approach: Eliphaz is relatively measured and seems to feel genuine compassion; Bildad is blunt and indignant; Zophar is the most brutal, mocking Job and telling him he deserves more suffering than he has received. All three are wrong, as God declares in chapter 42.

The teacher explains the dramatic role the friends play in the book's plot. They are not merely misguided counselors; they function as Satan's legal team, tasked with convincing Job that God is unjust and that he must have sinned to deserve his suffering. When physical affliction failed to turn Job from God, Satan deployed theological attack through the mouths of trusted friends. This makes chapters 4-27 a sustained illustration of how dangerous retribution theology is — and why so much biblical real estate is devoted to refuting it.

The lesson concludes with practical application: true biblical counsel sometimes wounds, but like a surgeon's scalpel it heals. The church must give and receive counsel that is rooted in Scripture and contextual, neither dismissing hard words as the counsel of Job's friends nor offering the kind of counsel that drives people away from God.
transcript:
## Introduction: Chapters 4 Through 27

We're back to Job. We're going to take two Wednesday evenings to look at chapters 4 through 27, and then another Wednesday to examine Elihu's contribution (which follows chapter 31), and then two or three weeks to look at God's response — God versus Job, which is how the book ends, with Job being tried before God rather than God being tried before Job as Job had hoped.

Chapters 1 and 2 of Job provide a rare peak behind the curtains of heaven. You can think of other places in the Bible where God opens the doors of heaven — Isaiah 6, the transfiguration, Revelation 4 and 5 — but Job 1 and 2 are among the most detailed glimpses we have of a conversation between God and Satan. And what is important to remember is that Job does not know that conversation took place. The friends don't know it either. We know it — and that gives us a completely different perspective on the rest of the book.

That conversation between God and Satan resulted in great suffering for Job. Satan's hypothesis — the one he is trying to prove — is not merely that Job will curse God. He is really trying to prove that God is not worthy of worship. He says, in effect: *The only reason Job worships You is because of the temporal blessings You have given him. Remove those blessings and he will curse You, because You, God, are not worthy of worship.*

Satan was proven wrong, at least in these first two chapters. Look at Job 2:9–10 — Job's wife urges him to curse God and die, and Job responds:

> *"Shall we indeed accept good from God, and shall we not accept adversity?"* (Job 2:10)

And the text tells us: in all this, Job did not sin with his lips.

## The Three Friends and Their Entry

At the end of chapter 2, Job's three friends are introduced:

> *"Now when Job's three friends heard of all this adversity that had come upon him, each one came from his own place — Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite. For they had made an appointment together to come and mourn with him, and to comfort him."* (Job 2:11)

They planned this. They came from a distance, gathered together, and went to comfort their suffering friend. So far, really good. But when they saw him from afar, they did not even recognize him — so profound were the physical effects of his suffering, reminiscent of Isaiah 53's description of the Suffering Servant.

Their initial response was admirable:

> *"So they sat down with him on the ground seven days and seven nights, and no one spoke a word to him, for they saw that his grief was very great."* (Job 2:13)

They sat in silence with Job for a full week. That was exactly what was needed. Sadly, the Geneva Bible's marginal note about these friends is telling: "These friends came unto him under pretense of consolation, and yet tormented him more than did all his afflictions." The real suffering, according to those scholars, began when the friends opened their mouths.

## Who Are These Three Men?

We are given their names and hometowns, but we know almost nothing about who they were. The best guess is that Eliphaz was from Edom — his name is an Edomite name, and there is an Eliphaz in Genesis 36. Edom was descended from Esau and was frequently hostile to Israel. Bildad is thought to be from the region near the Euphrates, somewhere in modern Iraq. Zophar — nobody knows where he was from. His name doesn't appear anywhere else in the literature of the period.

What we do know is their theology — specifically their shared commitment to what scholars call **retribution theology**: the idea that if you do good things, good things happen to you; if you do bad things, bad things happen to you. The book of Proverbs teaches this as a general principle. But retribution theology — the heresy these friends hold — takes it as an absolute law: the righteous never suffer, and the wicked are never blessed. If you are suffering, you must have sinned.

It is interesting that Eliphaz actually charges Job with hypocrisy, claiming that Job himself had counseled suffering people using exactly this framework:

> *"Your words have upheld him who was stumbling, and you have strengthened the feeble knees; but now it comes upon you, and you are weary."* (Job 4:4–5)

In other words: "Job, you used to tell people that their suffering was the result of their sin. Now you are suffering, and you refuse to repent. You are a hypocrite."

Bildad comes in harder than Eliphaz — no kindness, straight to the point:

> *"How long will you speak these things, and the words of your mouth be like a strong wind? Does God subvert judgment? Or does the Almighty pervert justice?"* (Job 8:2–3)

He cannot understand why Job won't accept what seems to him irrefutable: suffering equals personal unrighteousness. Bildad treats Job like a heretic for refusing to accept this.

Zophar is the rudest of the three:

> *"Should not the multitude of words be answered? And should a man full of talk be vindicated? Should your empty talk make men hold their peace?"* (Job 11:2–3)

He even argues that Job hasn't suffered *enough* — that God is actually extracting from him less than his iniquity deserves. Each cycle of dialogue repeats the same argument in different words. They never change their position.

## Two Questions About These Chapters

### 1. Why Are These Chapters Here?

Why does so much of Job — chapters 4 through 27 — consist of these painful dialogues? It seems to be that these chapters effectively display how foolish the idea is that the righteous never suffer. God spends so much space in this book to show how wrong and how toxic this theology is. The repetition is deliberate. We need to see the argument demolished again and again.

> *"Yes, and all who desire to live godly in Christ Jesus will suffer persecution."* (2 Timothy 3:12)

The righteous *do* suffer. The friends' theology is refuted by the very existence of Christ.

### 2. What Role Do the Three Friends Play?

These three friends become, unknowingly, **Satan's legal team**. When the suffering itself failed to turn Job away from God, Satan sent counselors to challenge Job's theology of who God is. They are trying to convince Job that God is unjust — that if Job hasn't sinned, then God must be arbitrary or unfair. That is exactly Satan's strategy.

Satan followed the same tactic with Jesus — he tried to convince Jesus to turn away in the wilderness, and when that failed, he used Peter: *"Far be it from You, Lord; this shall not happen to You!"* (Matthew 16:22). Satan brought people who were close to Jesus to try to turn Him from His mission.

We must be very careful that the counsel we give to one another is biblically accurate and contextually appropriate. These friends came to offer help that was designed — whether they knew it or not — to hurt. We must never be their counterpart. At the same time, we must not label every friend who brings us a hard word "Job's friends" simply because we don't want to hear it. The wounds of a friend are faithful. A spirit-filled friend who brings wise counsel may hurt — but it is the hurt of a surgeon's scalpel, which creates healing. The judgment of all counsel must always be what the Scripture says concerning the matter — not what our hearts want to do.

## Conclusion

In two weeks we'll pick up looking at how Job handled his friends' counsel. Next week, Jim will be leading Bible study here.

Let's pray. Father, we thank you for this word. Help us to be godly, good counselors, pointing people to you rather than away from you. Help us also to be counsel-receivers, so that we may be pointed to Christ as well. Thank you for giving us a body of believers that can help us all grow in Christ. May we be faithful to one another — so that even when we hurt each other in counsel, it would be the wounds of a friend, which are faithful. In Jesus' name, amen.
